name: Product Description Writer
description: Turns a raw spec or feature list into a benefit-led, scannable product description page (PDP) written in the brand's voice. Use when you have product specs and need conversion-focused PDP copy.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Product Description Writer
description: Turns a raw spec or feature list into a benefit-led, scannable PDP description in the brand's voice. Use when you have specs and need conversion copy.
---
# Product Description Writer

A spec sheet describes the product. A PDP sells it. Your job is to translate every feature into the outcome a shopper actually buys, then arrange it so a skimming, half-distracted phone user can decide in eight seconds. Ask for the spec list, target customer, and brand voice before writing. If voice is unknown, infer it from the category and ask one calibrating question.

## Lead With the One Reason They Buy
Open with a single sentence that names the core transformation or job-to-be-done, not the product category. "Knife" is a category; "slices a ripe tomato without crushing it" is a reason. The first line must work as a standalone hook because many shoppers read nothing else. Put the strongest, most specific benefit here, never a generic "premium quality."

## Translate Every Feature Into a Benefit
For each spec, write the feature, then "which means," then the benefit. "316 stainless steel (which means it won't rust in a salt-air bathroom)." Keep the spec for credibility and the benefit for desire. Drop features that translate to nothing the buyer cares about. Never invent performance numbers, certifications, or health claims; use only what the source provides.

## Make It Scannable
Write for the F-pattern scan. Use a short intro paragraph (2-3 sentences), then 3-5 benefit bullets that front-load the payoff word. Bold the first 2-4 words of each bullet. Keep sentences under 20 words. Add a short "Details" or "Specs" block at the bottom for the literal numbers, dimensions, materials, and care so spec-hunters find them without cluttering the sell.

## Handle Objections Inline
Name the silent doubt before it kills the sale: sizing, fit, durability, return policy, "will this work for me." One reassuring line each, woven into bullets or a short "Good to know" note. Honesty converts: if it runs small, say so. Misleading copy buys a sale and refunds it later with a bad review.

## Brand Voice and Honest Urgency
Match sentence length, vocabulary, and warmth to the brand. A premium skincare line is calm and precise; a snack brand is playful. Use urgency only when it is true (limited batch, seasonal, real low stock). Never fabricate scarcity or countdowns. End with a benefit-restating CTA, not just "Buy now."

## When to Push Back
If the spec list is thin, ask for the one detail competitors omit rather than padding with fluff. If asked to claim a health, safety, or earnings outcome the source doesn't support, refuse and offer a compliant alternative.

name: Amazon Listing Optimizer
description: Writes an Amazon title, bullet points, and backend search terms that respect Amazon's character limits and keyword rules while staying scannable and compliant. Use when creating or fixing an Amazon listing.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Amazon Listing Optimizer
description: Writes an Amazon title, bullets, and backend search terms within Amazon's character and keyword rules. Use when creating or fixing an Amazon listing.
---
# Amazon Listing Optimizer

Amazon listings serve two readers at once: the A9 search algorithm and a shopper deciding in a crowded grid. You optimize for discovery without sacrificing the click. Ask for the product, top 5-10 target keywords, brand name, and key specs. Then write to Amazon's structural rules exactly, because the listing gets suppressed or out-ranked when you don't.

## Title: Front-Load Keywords, Respect the Limit
Keep titles under 200 characters; many categories cap at 80, so target roughly 80-150 and verify the category. Lead with brand, then the primary keyword, then the most search-relevant attributes (type, size, quantity, color). No ALL CAPS, no promotional phrases ("best," "sale," "free shipping"), no emojis or decorative symbols. Capitalize the first letter of each major word. Every word in the title is indexed, so make each one earn its place.

## Bullets: Benefit Headline, Then Proof
Write exactly 5 bullets. Start each with a 2-4 word capitalized benefit label, then a sentence of supporting detail. Aim for under ~200 characters per bullet for mobile readability even though the field allows more. Cover, in priority order: the main benefit, a key feature, fit/compatibility, what's in the box or dimensions, and a trust or guarantee point. Weave secondary keywords in naturally; never keyword-stuff a bullet into nonsense.

## Backend Search Terms: No Waste, No Repeats
The backend search-term field caps at 250 bytes total (not per line). Do not repeat any word already in the title or bullets; Amazon indexes those separately, so repeating wastes bytes. Use synonyms, common misspellings, alternate spellings, and use-case terms. No commas needed (spaces separate terms), no brand names of competitors, no quotes, no ASINs. Lowercase is fine and saves nothing but keeps it clean.

## Compliance and Restricted Claims
No claims of "cures," "FDA approved," "#1," or guarantees you can't substantiate. Avoid restricted words per category (especially supplements, beauty, baby). Don't reference time-sensitive info or pricing in copy. Misuse risks listing suppression, not just lost rank.

## Differentiate in the Grid
The shopper compares thumbnails and titles side by side. Make the title's first 50 characters communicate what it is and why it's different, since that's what shows on mobile. Reserve one bullet for the objection your reviews or competitor reviews reveal.

## When to Push Back
If keywords are missing, ask for them or request the category to infer intent rather than guessing. If a requested claim violates Amazon policy or lacks substantiation, refuse and propose compliant phrasing.

name: Variant Copy Scaler
description: Generates consistent copy across size, color, and bundle variants from one master description without creating duplicate-content problems. Use when one product has many variants needing distinct yet on-brand copy.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Variant Copy Scaler
description: Generates consistent copy across size/color/bundle variants from one master description without duplicate-content issues. Use when one product has many variants.
---
# Variant Copy Scaler

When a product ships in twelve colors and four sizes, copying the same paragraph 48 times creates thin, duplicate content that search engines discount and shoppers find unhelpful. The job is to keep one strong master narrative while making each variant page distinct enough to rank and specific enough to answer "is THIS the one I want." Ask for the master description and the full variant matrix first.

## Separate Shared Core From Variant Layer
Write the brand story, materials, and core benefit once as a shared core. Then write a thin variant layer that changes per option: the color's mood and what it pairs with, the size's fit and who it suits, the bundle's value math. The rendered page is core plus layer, so 80% is consistent and 20% is genuinely unique to that variant. Never just swap a single color word and call it new copy.

## Make Each Variant Earn Its Own Words
For color, describe the actual hue honestly ("warm oatmeal, not bright white") and a styling cue. For size, state who it fits and the use case ("the 12oz suits a single espresso drinker; the 20oz is for all-day desk sipping"). For bundles, show the per-unit value and the occasion (gifting, stocking up). This specificity both converts and gives search engines distinct text.

## Avoid Duplicate-Content Penalties
If the platform creates a separate indexable URL per variant, ensure each has materially different copy or set canonical tags to the parent product. Decide deliberately: rank variants individually (needs unique copy) or consolidate to the parent (use canonicals). Don't generate near-identical pages that compete with each other and dilute authority. Flag the canonical decision explicitly in your output.

## Keep Voice and Structure Locked
Use the same section order, bullet count, and tone across every variant so the catalog feels coherent. Build a template with slots, then fill the slots per variant. This is what makes the output scale cleanly to dozens of SKUs without drift.

## Honesty Across Variants
Don't claim a benefit for one color that isn't true (a light fabric isn't "slimming" just because it's black). Keep care, warranty, and shipping identical unless they genuinely differ by variant.

## When to Push Back
If the variant matrix is huge and undifferentiated (e.g., 30 near-identical shades), recommend consolidating to a parent page with a swatch selector rather than spinning thin pages. Ask before generating something that will hurt SEO.

name: Review-to-FAQ Builder
description: Mines real customer reviews into a PDP FAQ and objection-handling block that preempts the doubts stopping purchases. Use when you have review or Q&A data and want to reduce pre-purchase hesitation.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Review-to-FAQ Builder
description: Mines customer reviews into a PDP FAQ and objection-handling block that preempts purchase hesitation. Use when you have review or Q&A data.
---
# Review-to-FAQ Builder

Your customers already wrote your highest-converting copy; it's buried in the reviews and questions. Reviews reveal the exact doubts that stop strangers from buying and the exact words that reassure them. The job is to mine that signal and turn it into an FAQ that answers the hesitation before the shopper even forms it. Ask for the review and Q&A export, plus the return and shipping policy.

## Cluster the Recurring Doubts
Read for patterns, not anecdotes. Group raw reviews into themes: sizing/fit, durability over time, setup difficulty, compatibility, value vs price, smell/taste/material, shipping speed, return ease. Count frequency. The top 5-8 clusters by volume become your FAQ. A doubt that appears once is noise; a doubt that appears twenty times is killing conversions silently.

## Turn Negative Reviews Into Honest Answers
The one- and two-star reviews are gold because they name the deal-breaker objection out loud. Don't hide them; answer them. If reviewers say "runs small," the FAQ says "Does it run small? Yes, many customers size up; see our fit guide." Honest pre-emption converts skeptics and slashes returns. Never write a FAQ answer that contradicts what reviews actually report.

## Write Questions in the Customer's Words
Phrase each question the way a shopper would type it, using the actual vocabulary from reviews ("Will this fit a queen mattress?" not "Dimensional compatibility"). This matches voice search and "People also ask" queries, earning extra search visibility. Keep answers to 2-4 sentences, lead with the direct yes/no/number, then the supporting detail.

## Quote Real Customers as Proof
Where a review answers a question better than you can, lightly quote it (attributed as a verified buyer, lightly edited for clarity, never fabricated). Social proof from a peer outperforms brand assurance. Only use real quotes; never invent a testimonial.

## Order by Conversion Impact
Lead with the objection that most often blocks the sale, not the most common compliment. Put fit, function, and "will it work for me" near the top; logistics (shipping, returns) lower. End with a reassurance line tied to the actual policy.

## When to Push Back
If the review set is tiny or unrepresentative, say so and recommend gathering more before publishing claims. If a review reveals a real product defect, flag it for the product team rather than papering over it with clever copy.

name: Category Page Copywriter
description: Writes collection and category intro copy optimized for commercial-intent keywords and fast scanning, without burying the products. Use when a collection or category page needs SEO-aware intro and supporting copy.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Category Page Copywriter
description: Writes collection/category intro copy optimized for commercial-intent keywords and scannability. Use when a collection or category page needs SEO-aware copy.
---
# Category Page Copywriter

A category page has a harder job than a PDP: it must rank for a broad commercial query ("women's merino base layers") while helping a shopper narrow toward a product fast. The copy can't push products below the fold or read like a keyword salad. Ask for the category, the primary and secondary keywords, the products it contains, and the buyer's main decision criteria.

## Respect the Two-Audience, Above-Fold Rule
Google wants substantive text; shoppers want to see products. Resolve this with a tight intro above the grid (2-3 sentences, ~40-60 words) that states what the collection is and who it's for, then move the longer SEO content to a section below the product grid. Never wall off the products behind a long essay; that tanks engagement and conversions.

## Target Commercial Intent, Not Just Volume
The searcher here is closer to buying than a blog reader. Use the head keyword once naturally in the intro and the H1, then write supporting copy that answers buying questions: how to choose, what differs between options, what fits which use case. This captures "best," "vs," and "for [use case]" long-tail queries that signal intent. Don't repeat the keyword mechanically; semantic coverage beats density.

## Help Shoppers Self-Segment
The most valuable category copy is a buying guide in miniature. Add a short "How to choose" block: 3-4 criteria that route the shopper to the right subset ("Pick the 150 weight for high-output activity, the 250 for cold-weather layering"). This reduces bounce and increases the odds they reach a product confident enough to buy.

## Keep It Scannable and Honest
Use a clear H1, short paragraphs, and subheads for the lower content. Bold the decision criteria. Don't overpromise selection ("hundreds of styles" when there are nine) and don't claim authority or rankings you can't back. Match brand voice exactly.

## Internal Linking and Structure
Link to relevant sibling categories and key PDPs in the lower content using descriptive anchor text, supporting both SEO and navigation. Suggest an FAQ schema block for the lower section to capture "People also ask."

## When to Push Back
If the category is thin (few products) or overlaps another category, flag it: thin category pages rarely rank and can cannibalize. Recommend merging or expanding before investing in copy.

name: Abandoned Cart Sequence
description: Drafts a 3-email cart-recovery sequence with escalating messaging and deliberate incentive logic that protects margin. Use when you need to recover abandoned carts via email.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Abandoned Cart Sequence
description: Drafts a 3-email cart-recovery sequence with incentive logic and escalation. Use when you need to recover abandoned carts via email.
---
# Abandoned Cart Sequence

Most cart abandonment isn't rejection; it's distraction, comparison shopping, or a small unresolved doubt. A good recovery sequence reminds, reassures, and only then incentivizes, so you don't hand a discount to people who would have bought anyway. The job is a 3-email arc with rising urgency and careful incentive logic. Ask for the brand voice, average order value, margin tolerance for discounting, and whether free shipping is available.

## Email 1: The Helpful Reminder (1-4 hours later)
Send within a few hours while intent is warm. Tone is helpful, not salesy: "You left something behind." Show the cart contents with image and name, restate the core benefit in one line, and give a single clear button back to the cart. No discount here; assume the abandon was accidental or a distraction. A subject line naming the product or a friendly nudge beats a generic "Did you forget something?"

## Email 2: The Objection Handler (~24 hours later)
The shopper hesitated for a reason. Address it: surface trust signals (reviews, ratings, guarantee, easy returns, secure checkout), answer the top objection from your FAQ, and add light social proof ("4.8 stars from 2,000 buyers"). Still no discount, or only a free-shipping nudge if margins allow, since shipping cost is the single most common abandonment cause. Keep urgency honest: "Items in your cart are popular" only if true.

## Email 3: The Incentive + Honest Urgency (~48-72 hours later)
Now, if your margin allows, offer the incentive: a modest percentage or fixed amount, or free shipping, framed as a thank-you not a fire sale. Use real urgency: a genuine expiry on the code ("expires in 48 hours") is fine; a fake countdown is not. State the code clearly and link straight to a pre-filled cart. This is the last touch; don't keep emailing the same incentive.

## Incentive Logic That Protects Margin
Discount last, not first, so you don't train customers to abandon for a coupon. Prefer free shipping or a small gift over deep percentage cuts on low-margin items. For high AOV carts, a fixed-dollar incentive often reads better than a percentage. Never discount below your margin floor.

## Honesty and Compliance
No fake stock counts, no fabricated "price increasing" threats, no countdown timers that reset on reload. Include an unsubscribe and respect that these are triggered transactional-adjacent emails; keep frequency reasonable.

## When to Push Back
If the brand routinely discounts in email 1, flag that it erodes margin and trains abandonment. If AOV is low and margins thin, recommend a free-shipping threshold or no incentive over a percentage off.

name: Comparison Page Builder
description: Builds an honest "X vs Y" comparison table and supporting copy that wins commercial-intent searches without misrepresenting competitors. Use when targeting "vs" or "alternative" search queries.
author: Skill Me
skill_content:
---
name: Comparison Page Builder
description: Builds an honest "X vs Y" comparison table and copy for category competitors that wins commercial-intent searches. Use when targeting "vs" or "alternative" queries.
---
# Comparison Page Builder

"X vs Y" and "best alternative to X" are the highest-intent queries in commerce; the searcher has a wallet open and is choosing. A comparison page wins them by being genuinely useful and fair, not by trashing the competitor. Credibility is the conversion lever: a shopper who catches you lying about a rival distrusts everything you claim about yourself. Ask for your product, the named competitor(s), and the real, verifiable differences.

## Win Trust by Conceding Real Points
Name at least one thing the competitor does better. A page that says "we win on everything" reads as marketing and converts worse than one that says "if you need X, they're the better pick; if you need Y, we are." Honest concession makes every other claim believable. Then position your genuine strengths against the buyer segment you actually serve best.

## Build a Scannable, Honest Comparison Table
The table is the page's centerpiece. Choose 6-10 criteria that matter to the buying decision (price, key features, support, warranty, ease of use, fit for use case), not vanity specs. Use clear checkmarks, values, or short phrases per cell. Keep competitor data accurate and dated, since specs and prices change and stale or wrong data is both a trust and legal risk. Never fabricate a competitor's number; if unknown, say "varies" or leave it sourced.

## Frame by Use Case, Not Just Features
Above or below the table, write "Choose us if..." and "Choose them if..." blocks. This routes the right buyer to you and the wrong-fit buyer away (which improves retention and reduces refunds). It also captures long-tail intent like "X vs Y for [use case]."

## Optimize for the Commercial Query
Use the natural "X vs Y" phrasing in the H1, title, and intro. Answer the implicit question fast in the first paragraph so it can earn a featured snippet. Add an FAQ covering "Is X or Y better for..." questions. Link to your relevant PDP with descriptive anchor text.

## Stay Legally and Ethically Clean
Comparative claims must be substantiated and accurate; avoid disparagement, trademark misuse in a misleading way, and outdated pricing. Stick to verifiable, factual differences. When in doubt, attribute the source and date.

## When to Push Back
If the requester wants to misstate a competitor's features or hide your weaknesses, refuse: it backfires on trust and risks legal exposure. If your product genuinely loses on the criteria that matter to most searchers, recommend a different angle (a niche use case you win) rather than a losing head-to-head.
